summary:
The Great Wall of China in its original Qin dynasty form (c. 221–206 BC) was one of the most ambitious construction projects in ancient history — an attempt to link and extend existing northern border walls built by the Warring States kingdoms into a unified defensive barrier against the Xiongnu nomads of the steppe. Qin Shi Huang dispatched General Meng Tian with reportedly 300,000 laborers (soldiers, conscripted peasants, and convicts) to build the wall across the northern frontier.

The Qin wall ran roughly along the boundary between agricultural China and the pastoral steppe, following ridgelines and natural barriers. It was built of earth, stone, and wood depending on local materials, integrated with watchtowers and garrison posts. The human cost was enormous — tradition records that workers who died were incorporated into the wall (though this is probably legendary). The wall as built by the Qin was less extensive and less imposing than the better-known Ming dynasty Great Wall (1368–1644 AD), which is what most visitors see today. Nevertheless, the Qin wall established the concept and general line that all subsequent dynasties would modify, extend, and rebuild. The [[event:1249|Great Wall Extension Han]] extended the system westward, and the wall remained an intermittently maintained feature of Chinese frontier defense for over 1,500 years.
